data.title: Greenland: We Have A Problem
data.description:
Just when it looked like markets were calming down, the tariff-related drama is rattling markets again. As of early Monday morning, the S&P 500 futures "SPX" (SP500) were down by roughly 1%, the Nasdaq 100 futures were down by about 1.5%, gold was up by about 2%, and silver was surging by about 5%.

Remarkably, silver futures hit nearly $95 in early Monday trade, creeping ever closer to that $100 mark. In any case, the risk-off feel is back, as President Trump makes claims to "acquire" Greenland and has even put higher tariffs back on the table for several European nations.

We already learned that the market wasn't crazy about tariffs early last year. So, we're also seeing yields rising, with the 10-year above 4.2%, and the 30-year jacked up back above 4.8% on Monday morning. Now, how are mortgage and other key rates supposed to come down with Treasuries trending higher again? Additionally, the Europeans aren't sitting on their hands here, and the response has been quite unified and negative over the escalation regarding Greenland. Now, the EU is contemplating answering with a $100+ billion tariff package to counter the U.S. tariff threat.

Also, the EU owns a lot of U.S. debt, stocks, and many other assets, and its U.S. Treasury holdings could be Europe's "big stick," Dutch Bank thinks. Theoretically, EU countries could dump their U.S. Treasuries and other assets, potentially causing volatility to spike, especially in the near term here.

Moreover, it's now becoming clearer that Kevin Warsh may be named as the next Fed Chair, which is considerably less dovish than the appointment of Kevin Hassett would be, in my view.

Earnings season, economic indicators, and an upcoming FOMC event are additional factors that may increase near-term uncertainty, which may lead to increased volatility.

However, despite these near-term issues potentially leading to more transitory volatility in the near term, I remain bullish in the intermediate and longer term.

The resilient economic backdrop, continued expansion in AI and other crucial segments, easier monetary policy, lower taxes, deregulation, and other constructive elements should drive high-quality stocks higher in 2026. Therefore, I am keeping my 2026 year-end S&P 500 target at 8,000.

Technically: Near-Term Volatility Could Increase

The SPX is around a critical support area, which is at 6,900-6,800. Now, with the increase in volatility, the SPX may even temporarily dip below 6,800. However, if the 6,700 support is breached, the SPX and markets in general could go through a more extensive correction process. Provided the setup, if 6,700 support is breached, we may see a pullback down to roughly 6,500-6,300 in a worst-case outcome.

Volatility Over Greenland

Markets hate uncertainty, and the tariff escalation over the situation in Greenland introduces significant near-term uncertainties. The U.S. President appears intent on acquiring the island, and unless a compromise with the EU allies is achieved soon, this situation could disrupt financial markets. While a repeat of last April's meltdown isn't likely, in my view, a 5-10% correction could potentially occur.

Earnings Season To Heat Up

We've seen several key reports, and most big banks, as well as other early bellwether companies, have reported better than anticipated top and especially bottom line results. This week, we have Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), 3M Company (MMM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), The Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW), General Electric Company (GE), and other critical companies reporting.

Then, we get to peak earnings season around the end of January and the beginning of next month. Positive tech and other critical earnings could serve as a positive catalyst to limit the geopolitical-related uncertainty and potentially reflect positively on high-quality stocks.

Upcoming Crucial Data

We have key, crucial data this week, especially on Wednesday and Thursday. The President's speech on Wednesday morning may be a market-moving event. Also, we have GDP, PCE, and other essential data coming in this week, which could move markets. Especially important is the core PCE, as we would prefer to see lower than expected inflation. A 2.7% would be okay, but I believe the market would really like to see 2.6%, which could increase near-term rate cut probabilities.

The Bottom Line: Valuations Could Expand

While the trailing P/E ratios may appear elevated, the forward P/E ratios are not, provided the potential solid growth and the easier monetary environment. The SPX has a forward P/E ratio of 22, which is relatively inexpensive, provided the environment and the economic backdrop. Moreover, the Nasdaq 100 forward P/E ratio is only about 25, and the Russell 2000 appears even cheaper, trading only around 24 times forward earnings estimates.

Excluding the temporary geopolitical elements and other transitory factors, multiples could expand in a solid or even moderate growth environment coupled with a more accessible monetary policy. In addition, there are favorable growth areas like AI, defense, energy, and others that could drive economic growth and corporate earnings growth. Therefore, stocks have a high probability to trend higher, and I am keeping my 8,000 SPX target for year-end 2026.

data.title: Nebius: I Was All Wrong About AI Infrastructure Demand (Upgrade)
data.description:
When I covered Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) last time, I was quite confident that it was an easy "Strong Sell" because it seemed too expensive at $11+ billion market cap. At the moment, Nebius Group is worth more than $27 billion, meaning that my call was all wrong. The stock skyrocketed after the massive AI infrastructure deal was announced in early September 2025. My bearish opinion was mostly based on the idea that NBIS is too small to compete with the U.S. and Chinese tech giants in the AI infrastructure business. However, it turned out that the demand is so high that even giants like Microsoft are not able to meet it without outsourcing. There are numerous recent developments showing us that AI infrastructure demand is poised to accelerate further as the number of AI use cases is expanding rapidly. Valuation remains the major risk due to high execution risks, but there is a massive share price momentum on the other hand. All in all, I plan to open a relatively small NBIS position and will be doubling down in case of notable sentiment-driven dips. All in all, I am upgrading NBIS to "Buy".

Recent developments

As I mentioned in the introduction, NBIS soared after the large AI infrastructure deal with Microsoft was announced. It happened in early September, meaning that it was already covered by many other Seeking Alpha analysts. So, I will share my opinion on it very briefly. A $17+ billion deal with the world's second largest cloud infrastructure player and one of the most iconic technological companies in history is not just about financials, but it is a massive validation victory for Nebius. By the way, Nebius also has a $3 billion AI infrastructure deal with Meta Platforms. It is almost six times smaller dollar-wise than the Microsoft deal, but it is still a multi-billion contract with another tech giant. I think that there is nothing bigger for a young technological company like NBIS from validation perspective other than a multi-billion contract with one of the companies representing the "Magnificent 7" group. It means that your company has been recognized as being in the same league as them from technological perspective. And Nebius already boasts two contracts with Mag 7 companies, not including deepening technological partnership with Nvidia.

Therefore, I am not surprised that NBIS enjoys such a strong share price momentum. The stock is up by staggering 415% over the last nine months, which is absolutely jaw-dropping. NBIS is down by roughly 12% over the last three months, but I think that this share price weakness was mostly sentiment-driven due to Nvidia's (NVDA) relatively weak share price performance over the same period. Moreover, there is still uncertainty about fairness of Nebius' valuation, which I will discuss in a separate section.

I think that the sell-off of the last three months was mostly sentiment-driven because industry developments were all positive for NBIS over the last few months. We have figured out at the beginning of this passage that with large deals with Microsoft and Meta, Nebius already boasts reputation as a five-star player in the AI infrastructure market. And the market is poised to continue growing rapidly given several recent developments. During the CES 2026 event, Nvidia's management made several bullish statements regarding still strong AI-related demand. Their positive outlook was recently reinforced by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's (TSM) strong AI-driven Q4 earnings together with the fact that Taiwan is set to invest at least $250 billion in semiconductor, energy, and AI production in the U.S.

There are other indications that the world needs much more compute power for AI, which we can see from recent statements from prominent AI memory chip players like Micron (MU) and Samsung Electronics (SSNLF). Micron recently said that the global memory chip shortage is "unprecedented", and it is likely to last beyond 2026. Top managers of Samsung are also very bullish as they expect to see a 3x YoY increase in Q4 2025 operating profit thanks to the booming AI-related memory chip business. Having such bullish statements from various large players in the industry is unlikely to be just hype or something unsustainable. I think that the logic behind soaring AI compute power is really simple. The size of AI infrastructure market will depend on the number of use cases for artificial intelligence, and this number appears to be growing quite aggressively.

BCG's "AI Radar 2026" is a quite interesting survey, which I consider representative, as it is a comprehensive research across various countries, industries, and company sizes. It researched views of 2,360 respondents, mostly C-suite executives. Therefore, I think that the source is highly reliable in assessing the power of AI momentum. Apart from the key takeaways summarized in the above slide, I also believe it is crucial to mention that the report also cites that the survey cites a 2x forecasted increase in AI-related investments [on average from ~0.8% of revenue in 2025 to ~1.7% in 2026]. Such a significant expected spike in terms of AI investments likely means that business leaders see great potential to improve processes within their companies by incorporating AI capabilities. Since the survey covers companies from various industries, it is highly likely that the number of use AI cases has great potential to expand further.

With such strong industry tailwinds, I expect Nebius to deliver new large positive surprises. Every new large AI infrastructure contract will highly likely lead to substantial upgrades from Wall Street analysts to Nebius' earnings estimates.

Valuation remains a major risk despite the upgrade

While I believe that massive share price momentum together with accelerating AI adoption is a powerful combination to push NBIS share price significantly higher, I am still concerned about its valuation. A $27+ billion market cap for a company with negative EPS is a very generous valuation even though Nebius' Price/Sales ratio is expected to compress dramatically over the next few years. The fact that Nebius already has contracts with Microsoft and Meta cumulatively worth more than $20 billion makes a projected rapid P/S ratio contraction highly probable.

However, Nebius doesn't have a proven record of converting aggressive revenue growth into bottom line profitability. While revenue growth is a vital metric for a tech company, it might make no difference for investors over the long-term if NBIS fails to deliver operational leverage. Current generous valuation of Nebius implies that investors expect the company to generate superior returns over the long term.

The biggest risk at this stage of development is execution risk. First and foremost, delivering quality and value for Microsoft and Meta means that Nebius has no room for material error during the AI infrastructure buildout phase. Not meeting schedules agreed with its largest customers might lead to deteriorated business relationships and flawed reputation. Moreover, missing deadlines and budgets will also mean that the company's actual profits from these large deals might be lower-than-expected. Due to the "priced to perfection status" of NBIS investors might punish the stock with large selloffs if there are doubts about the company's ability to keep its largest customers happy.

Bottom line

To conclude, I think that NBIS has potential to soar further given massive share price momentum together with positive developments around the AI infrastructure industry. On the other hand, valuation fairness is a big question, which might be a massive red flag if the entire U.S. equity market decides it is time to play defensive. Therefore, assigning a "Buy" rating together with initiating a relatively small position is a reasonable strategy.

data.title: Microsoft: The $134 Billion Misunderstanding
data.description:
Headlines emerged on Saturday that Elon Musk is seeking damages of $79B-$134B from OpenAI (OPENAI) and Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), alleging that the maker of ChatGPT defrauded the Tesla CEO (NASDAQ:TSLA). The case is fast-tracking to an April jury trial in Oakland, with Musk claiming his 2015 donation of $38M entitles him to partial ownership of the for-profit OpenAI. In 2015, OpenAI had non-profit status; however, in 2019 a for-profit subsidiary was launched with the OpenAI Foundation remaining non-profit.

Legal concerns notwithstanding, investors should look past the headline noise. Filings reveal that Microsoft's exposure is capped at a maximum of ~$25B. While a considerable sum, this figure represents roughly two months of operating cash flow or less than 25% of the company's $102B war chest comprised of cash, equivalents, and short-term investments.

While Microsoft owns 27% of OpenAI, the primary target of the lawsuit is OpenAI. Thus, the real risk to Microsoft is not a fine but a potential delay to OpenAI's IPO. At present, the market's reaction has created a disconnect between sentiment and fundamentals.

Despite the headline risk, now it is time to buy the dip on this 'Magnificent 7' leader. While the stock has declined approximately 20% from its highs, catalysts have emerged, including:

  • Valuation Support: Trading near $455/share, Microsoft is valued at 28.3x 2026 earnings with an anticipated 19.2% in earnings growth. This valuation represents the low end of its recent historical range, presenting investors with a good margin of safety.
  • Growth: Microsoft remains the premier hyperscaler, with 16% revenue growth anticipated in 2026. With the AI boom still in its early innings, the company has ample runway in Azure, Cloud, and Co-Pilot to fuel growth in the coming years.
  • Sentiment Washout: Following the Michael Burry 'big short' thesis on AI and the recent lawsuit, Microsoft is approaching a bottom in sentiment. The lawsuit concern is capped, and I believe Burry is wrong or early on his AI bubble call, presenting investors with a contrarian entry.
  • Earnings Catalyst: Expectations for Q2 2026 earnings (Jan. 28th) present a low bar for revenue ($80.3B) and EPS ($3.86/share). If Microsoft follows last year's seasonality and sustains its 33% Azure growth, a beat is highly probable.
  • OpenAI IPO: Microsoft's 27% stake in OpenAI remains a potential upside catalyst. With a $1T IPO rumored for late 2026 or early 2027, the repricing of this asset is currently not reflected in the stock price.

In total, the present valuation and catalysts for the coming year position Microsoft as a Strong Buy. Shares offer 12-month upside to $600/share, a potential 32% return.

Microsoft: Q4 2025 Earnings Preview

For the coming quarterly earnings report, the bar is set too low. In the most recent quarter, Microsoft saw revenue expand to $77.7 billion, with diluted GAAP EPS reaching $3.72/share (up 17% and 13% YoY). The table below (from the latest 10-Q) shows the most recent growth in Microsoft's business segments.

The current consensus estimates call for $80.3 billion in revenue and $3.86/share in EPS. Microsoft's historical seasonality suggests fiscal Q2 (ending in Jan.) is generally the strongest due to year-end budget flushing and strength in the holiday period. In Q2 2025, revenue increased 12% YoY to $69.6 billion, while EPS increased 10% to $3.23/share (6.1% QoQ sales growth from Q1 2025). Thus, in the coming quarter, Wall Street projects a sales increase of $2.6 billion from the $77.7 billion reported in September (3.3% QoQ growth). If Microsoft matches its typical seasonal velocity, revenue would land near $82.4 billion, clearing the consensus bar by over $2 billion.

The primary engine for this beat is Azure. In Q3 2025, Azure growth accelerated to 40%, debunking the "deceleration" narrative that plagued the stock in recent months. Microsoft's net margin in Q2 2025 was 34.6%, recording $24.1 billion in net income from revenues of $69.6 billion.

The math for the bottom line is tighter than the top line. To hit consensus $3.86/share EPS targets, Microsoft needs a slight margin expansion to 34.8% with share count constant since last year ($82.4 billion in revenue at 34.8% margin realizes $28.5 billion in net income or $3.86/share with 7.4324 billion shares). While possible, expect investors to monitor Capex changes closely for clues. Increasing capital investment spending is a risk to the stock in the coming quarter, as investors have reacted negatively to any increase.

Microsoft Sentiment: The Musk and Burry Discount

The headline risk of Elon Musk’s seeking up to $134 billion in damages harms sentiment, but the financial reality is far less concerning. Court filings clarify that Microsoft’s specific exposure is capped between $13.3 billion and $25.1 billion.

To put this "worst-case" scenario in perspective, we turn to Microsoft’s balance sheet. As of Sept. 2025, the company held $102 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. Thus, the company could pay the maximum estimated damages four times over without touching its credit lines. From another perspective, Microsoft realized $34.2 billion in operating cash flow in just the last quarter.

While I am not a legal expert, Musk's claim of up to $134 billion in damages (13.4% of a potential $1T IPO) seems disproportionate to his $38 million investment. While 2015 was very early (representing up to 60% of OpenAI's seed funding), Microsoft has committed up to "$13 billion, with $11.6 billion funded as of Sept. 30, 2025," according to the latest 10-Q. The disparity between the headline number and the actual investment suggests the final settlement will likely be a fraction of what Musk is seeking. Finally, the jury will be selected in Oakland, California, which may bode poorly for Mr. Musk.

Beyond the legal noise, Microsoft is suffering from poor sentiment following Michael Burry’s "Big Short" thesis waged against the AI sector in late 2025. The trade, which predicted an AI infrastructure collapse and specifically targeted NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA), has so far failed to materialize as hyperscaler revenues have accelerated. Unless AI fears correctly anticipate a decline in the growth of this new technology, this creates a classic contrarian entry point.

The adoption curve supports this view, with ChatGPT recently reporting 700 million weekly active users. However, internal projections target 2.6 billion active weekly users by 2030. With Microsoft owning 27% of this asset, the current share price offers investors a 'fairly valued' stock to capitalize on the AI boom.

Microsoft: Analyst Growth Expectations and Reverse DCF Implied Growth

On a number of metrics, Microsoft is nearing a nadir in valuation compared to the past 5 years. At 32.7x trailing earnings, it is cheaper than any time except the 2022 market bottom and last year's 'tariff tantrum.' Price to sales and EV/EBITDA paint a similar picture, reinforcing that the stock may be close to a bottom and is worth watching closely for positive momentum.

While 32.7x trailing 12-month earnings represents a premium to the S&P 500, this higher multiple discounts the increased growth that the market is expecting. Wall Street analysts see 16% top-line growth in 2026 and continuing double-digit growth in revenues until 2030.

As Microsoft's revenue grows, analysts see gradually rising margins. As the company grows, its economy of scale will become stronger, fueling 19.2% EPS growth in 2026 and nearly 20% growth for the remainder of the decade. If analysts are correct, Microsoft will earn $16.26/share in fiscal 2026, corresponding to a forward earnings multiple of 28.3x and a PEG ratio of 1.47x.

In an attempt to model what level of growth the market is pricing into Microsoft's shares, a reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model was constructed. The net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) was calculated to be TTM operating income ($135.94B) less the current tax rate (19.0%) growing for the next 5 years at the analyst-projected revenue growth rate (13-16%). Depreciation and amortization ($98.88B TTM) was set to grow at 3% annually.

Capex spending is the most difficult to estimate. As of the most recent quarter, Microsoft estimated Capex to be $34.9B but guided that it would "increase sequentially" throughout the year. Analysts have modeled full-year Capex closer to $145B-$150B, assuming the quarterly spend ramps up toward $40B per quarter.

To anticipate that Microsoft is a good investment, there must be normalization of Capex spending. If the company spends $150B and we extrapolate this trend higher, it will dwarf free cash flow and greatly decrease the fair value of shares. The current surge in capital expenditures cannot be permanent, and for this model, it is assumed to be roughly half chips (which are shorter-lived) and half data centers (which should be longer-lived). On the latest earnings call, the company stated:

Capital expenditures were $34.9 billion driven by growing demand for our cloud and AI offerings. This quarter, roughly half of our spend was on short-lived assets, primarily GPUs and CPUs, to support increasing Azure platform demand, growing first-party apps and AI solutions, accelerating R&D by our product teams, as well as continued replacement for end-of-life server and networking equipment. The remaining spend was for long-lived assets that will support monetization for the next 15 years and beyond, including $11.1 billion of finance leases that are primarily for large datacenter sites.

Thus, in the following 5 years, the Capex was normalized to decline sequentially from $150B to $75B, wherein by 2030 the spending will be focused on upgrading chips in the company's data centers.

A discount rate of 9% was applied, with an intermediate period from 2031 to 2039 seeing 5% growth and a terminal growth rate of 2% thereafter. After adding cash minus liabilities, this approximates the current value of Microsoft's shares ($463/share).

FCFF (billions) 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030 2031-2039 Terminal
Implied Growth 16.0% 15.1% 16.2% 13.0% 13.9% 5% 2%
Revenue 326.88 376.17 437.03 493.89 562.53
NOPAT (33.7% of revenues) 110.11 126.73 147.28 166.44 189.57
+D&A (increasing 3% annually) 98.88 101.85 104.90 108.05 111.29
-CAPEX 150.00 131.25 112.50 93.75 75.00
=FCFF 63.99 97.33 139.68 180.74 225.86
Discount Factor 0.91 0.828 0.754 0.686 0.624
Net Present Value 58.23 80.59 105.32 123.99 140.94 1,200 1,670
Sum 3,379
Cash Minus Long-term Debt 66.63
Total Value 3,445.6
Shares Outstanding 7,432.4
Price per Share $463.00

My conclusion is that Microsoft is fairly priced, with faster (13-16%) growth until 2030, moderate growth (5%) in the 2030s, and a terminal growth rate of 2% thereafter. This is provided that the current capital investment in data centers is front-loaded with the company spending on chips and breaking ground on data centers that will be gradually reduced to only require upgrading the chips at later stages.

Thus, I concur with Michael Burry's analysis that the present rate of increase in Capex spending is unsustainable. However, I believe that the rate of spending will moderate over time. The market likely assumes the same, or else the rate of spending would have pushed shares down more significantly. Although Microsoft appears fairly valued in this context, a chief risk is spending on data centers. Investors should expect that the market will be very attentive to increases or decreases in Capex spending.

Conclusion

The rally in Microsoft is not dead, merely resting. The market is currently offering this 'Magnificent 7' compounder at a discount due to concerns over slowing growth in AI, Capex spending and legal headline risks. The washout in AI sentiment since Burry's 'Big Short' thesis presents an excellent contrarian opening to purchase shares of the hyperscaler Microsoft around its lowest valuation since the AI trade became widely known.

Buying Microsoft offers diversification from a more established 'wide moat' business in personal computing, with a fortress balance sheet presenting $102 billion in liquidity. In the coming 12-months it is likely that the Musk lawsuit will pass and the market will instead be looking forward to the coming OpenAI IPO, providing a powerful catalyst for shares given Microsoft's 27% ownership stake.

As a result, Microsoft is a Strong Buy at today's prices, presenting investors with potential 32% upside to $600/share in the coming year.

data.title: Powell Industries: Valuation Catches Up As Growth Moderates (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The Thesis

Exiting FY25, provider of custom-engineered electrical power control and distribution solutions Powell Industries (NASDAQ: POWL) delivered another quarter marking moderating growth due to softness across the core oil & gas sector. While the backlog remains solid near a record $1.4 billion and order momentum continued in the electric utility sector, the company's consolidated topline growth is expected to remain subdued through FY26 as overall order activity remains soft primarily in core sectors due to lower commercial activity.

For margins, however, I think pricing strength and a focus on strong project execution should remain key growth drivers in the quarters ahead. Since my last bullish article on POWL a few months back, the POWL stock has been up in strong double digits. However, the recent surge has made the valuation unreasonable, given the company's forward growth expectations. Therefore, despite the company's strong fundamentals and a promising long-term outlook, I think current valuation levels of the stock alter the risk-reward profile. Considering this, I am downgrading the POWL stock to a hold for now.

POWL’s Q4 2025 Highlights

Throughout FY25, growth across both POWLs’ top and bottom lines has meaningfully moderated due to persisting softness in its traditional end markets, Oil & Gas. After a low double-digit growth in the first half, POWL exited the year with approximately 8.4% year on year growth across its consolidated topline to $298 million in Q4.

While the softness across the core Oil & Gas sector continued, as shown by a significant 25% decline in its revenue during the last quarter, the single-digit growth was primarily a result of continued strength in the Electric Utility sector, which grew over 100% in Q4. Meanwhile, the Petrochemical and the Commercial and Other Industrial sectors were down by 10% and 9%, respectively, during the quarter, mainly due to tougher comparisons and project timing differences. For the full year 2025, the consolidated topline expanded 9.1%, reaching a record $1.1 billion.

Although pressure across the company’s topline continued as the company exited the second half, its profitability improved notably during the quarter as its operating margins expanded 80 bps to 21.2% as compared to the prior year’s Q4. This improvement was primarily driven by a healthy mix of large projects and strong execution, which offsets the impact from higher SG&A during the quarter. Certain favorable project closeouts also contributed to this margin expansion. Increased operating income also continue to support steady bottom-line growth, as the company’s adjusted EPS increased to $4.22 from $3.77 a year ago, surpassing the consensus estimate by a notable $0.44 in Q4 FY25.

Outlook

After a strong double-digit closing to FY24, POWL exited FY25 showing a clear moderation in growth across its consolidated topline as pace slowed meaningfully in core sectors, including oil & gas. While a notable part of growth moderation in the past few quarters resulted from project timing difference and tougher comparisons from a solid FY24, project activity also appears to have normalized in key sectors, as shown in the order momentum during the year, which kept the total backlog nearly flat versus the prior year at approximately $1.4 billion as of FY25 end.

The oil & gas and petrochemical sectors, which, despite a weak 2025, still account for about 50% of total revenue, continue to experience headwinds from soft commercial activity in the end markets. While the strength on the LNG side continues with a solid project pipeline and the company also continue to invest in capacity expansion to serve oil & gas customer over the coming years, I expect the sector revenue growth to remain under pressure with broader softness in the short term.

The collective share of oil & gas and petrochemical sectors in the total backlog has also declined to about 40% from 50% in FY24 due to a year of weak order activity as can be seen in the table below. While near-term pressure continued, order momentum is likely to remain weak through FY26, which could further weigh on the overall volumes and revenue in the quarters ahead.

Although core sectors were under pressure in the last quarter, strong performance in the company’s electric utility sector largely offsets the weakness by nearly doubling in Q4 as compared to the prior year. After a year of outsized growth, the electric utility business now accounts for approximately a 25% share in POWL’s consolidated revenue, up from about 19% in FY24. Moving into FY26, demand environment for the company’s electrical components and solutions remains robust, driven by grid modernization and transmission-related projects.

In my view, as utilities across North America continue to invest significantly to support rising electricity demand across the region, the order activity should remain robust over the coming quarters and help the company sustain strong double-digit growth in electric utility end market. However, given the pressure in the core sectors, the positive impact of electric utility growth should be limited in the short term.

Talking about the company’s other commercial and industrial end market, which includes the data center, revenue growth remained nearly flat in FY25 versus the prior year, primarily due to impact of project timing differences in the second half of FY25. However, underlying activity in this sector remains healthy with expanding penetration primarily across data center related applications. While the contribution from this end market may fluctuate in the short term, it should support overall topline through the later half of FY26 and also act as an important diversification level for POWL over the quarters ahead.

Overall, while POWL ended FY25 with a solid backlog of approximately $1.4 billion, which provides strong visibility into FY26, it is important to note that the order momentum has slowed down meaningfully with just 1% Y/Y order growth in Q4. With the company’s book-to-bill ratio now dipping below 1.0x, it appears that new orders are just replacing revenue. Backlog is indeed not collapsing, but it is also not expanding meaningfully, which limits the growth visibility in my view. In my view, despite continued strength in certain parts of the business and visibility from backlog, continued pressure in the key sectors and broader order momentum slowdown are expected to continue to weigh on the consolidated topline growth as POWL enters FY26.

Valuation Update

I last covered the POWL stock in October, about a month ahead of its Q4 earnings release. I maintained my buy rating despite the recent rally due to its discounted valuation near 20x its FY25 earnings estimate. Since then, the stock has rallied more than 30% and is currently near $420, thanks to the recent surge in the stock price. Meanwhile, since my bullish call in May '25, the POWL stock has been up impressively by 140%, significantly outperforming the market, as the S&P 500 increased by about 22% during the same period.

However, the recent price surge has pushed valuation well ahead of the company’s slowing growth trajectory. Currently, the company's stock is trading at forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 27.58, based on its FY26 EPS estimate of $15.23. The valuation looks expensive when compared with its sector median multiple around 22x. In addition to this, the company's forward growth expectation has also declined meaningfully to single digits, as can be seen in the table, which further makes the valuation unreasonable at the current levels.

Going ahead, POWL's topline growth is expected to remain subdued in the short term despite backlog visibility, as softness in the core end markets continues and overall order activity continue to moderate. However, margin prospects look strong as pricing remains favorable and the company continue to focus on efficient project execution. Overall project mix has also improved in the past few quarters, which should further support margins over the coming quarters despite weak anticipated volume growth.

Importantly, the company remains completely debt-free, which is expected to continue to support bottom line growth given the strong margin profile. However, with modest topline growth expectations, I believe these strengths alone may not be sufficient to meaningfully support bottom line expansion, which might keep the valuation elevated. The combination of elevated valuation and moderating growth suggests that the risk-reward is now more balanced at current levels, which is expected to limit the stock's upside in the short term.

Final Verdict And A Rating Downgrade

As discussed above, after a decent rally, the POWL stock appears to be trading at a slight premium. As POWL enters FY26, its consolidated topline is expected to remain under pressure due to continued weakness across key sectors, including oil & gas and petrochemical. Meanwhile overall margins are expected to stay strong with solid project execution and a better mix.

At the current multiple, the POWL stock appears ahead of growth expectations. Therefore, despite a promising long-term outlook with structural tailwinds, the company's weak growth prospects in the short term make me take a step back from my bullish stance. Hence, I would like to downgrade the POWL stock and give it a "HOLD" rating until growth visibility improves meaningfully.

data.title: After Decades Of Stability, Greenland Will Put This Alliance To The Test
data.description:
Background

President Trump has had successive clashes with European leaders for a long time, and the lowest point in this complex relationship was the announcement of tariffs on Europeans in 2025. However, a new clash has taken on more dramatic contours.

President Trump gave several speeches where he highlighted how strategic Greenland is for American national security and for the creation of its golden dome for missile interception. Additionally, President Trump said he would take control of Greenland one way or another.

European leaders gave a subtle response, but one that could have triggered a dangerous escalation. Germany, France, Sweden, Norway, Finland, and the Netherlands sent military troops in small numbers to the Arctic island.

The response was quick; President Trump announced that Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, and Finland will have tariffs on products shipped to the US of 10% on February 1st. Additionally, rates will increase to 25% from June 1.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent came to the defense of the American president. He stated that he believes that the Europeans will understand that the best result is for the United States to have control of Greenland. The former American vice president, Mike Pence, asked both to calm down, although he argued that Greenland is strategic for the USA.

The European Union is considering imposing around €93 million in tariffs in a reactivation of measures suspended after the trade agreement with the USA. Another possibility is the activation of the Anti-Coercion Instrument, known as the European Union's commercial bazooka. This is the background for you, reader, and now, let's analyze the possible impacts on investments.

Analyzing Scenarios

The situation is delicate, as according to the October report, only Germany, France, and the UK were responsible for 8.4% of the goods imported by the USA, and if we add the other countries targeted by tariffs, we should reach around 10%.

Some time ago I wrote about how control of Greenland would be strategic for the United States, not only because of its mineral wealth but also because of its strategic location in the geopolitical context (due to Russia's proximity and new Arctic shipping routes), especially in light of the National Security Strategy.

Since then I have not changed my opinion, and it is important that the reader understand that this is an analysis from an investment perspective and not from a political or moral perspective, as this is not my objective here. There is no doubt that controlling Greenland would increase American competitive advantages from the perspective of geopolitics and security, which could even contribute to the reduction in the CDS, which has an impact on the valuation of companies.

Therefore, if the Trump administration is successful in negotiations and controls Greenland, I see a great benefit for the United States; however, we will have other dangerous implications, which I consider major risks for the investment scenario. Negotiations could mean the implosion of NATO and of a system of alliances that has been very effective since the Second World War. This could make the world even more warlike.

In this context, I see a big tailwind in European defense and related companies such as WisdomTree Europe Defense Fund ETF (WDEF) and Select STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense ETF (EUAD). Additionally, in the short term, we should see a flight from risky assets to safe assets, such as hard assets led by gold.

The Davos Economic Forum is approaching and will be attended by President Trump. Given all the events, this promises to be the most important forum in recent years for world leaders.

I remind you that I have a weekly series on macroeconomics where I reiterate my positive view on the S&P 500, and recently I created a series on hard assets to consolidate my recommendations to buy gold, silver, and others. A few days ago I reiterated my recommendation to buy the XAR aerospace and defense ETF, but I will do a detailed analysis on European defense ETFs given this situation.

The Bottom Line

Greenland is once again in the spotlight, which shows the importance of geopolitics for the Trump administration. The fact is that my job is not to analyze the morality of the act, or even the geopolitical context, but rather to analyze the impacts of this on the investment scenario.

That said, this event alone should be a major catalyst for European defense companies and safe-haven assets like gold. In a second moment, control of Greenland could accentuate American competitive advantages on the global stage.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my bullish theses on the S&P 500, hard assets, and defensive ETFs. The scenario clearly points to countries strengthening their militaries, and this will have a clear impact on the asset classes described in this article.

data.title: Geopolitical Turmoil Won't End The Bull Market
data.description:
The headlines since the beginning of this year have done nothing but breed uncertainty on the geopolitical front, but rarely have such events upended the long-term trend in markets beyond instigating pullbacks or corrections that present buying opportunities. Financial markets have largely ignored the removal of President Maduro from Venezuela, threats to attack Iran, as well as concerns about Federal Reserve independence. The latest turmoil is more serious in that it involves a significant escalation in tensions with our trading partners and the possibility of higher levies, which could further curtail the rate of global economic growth.

Over the weekend, President Trump stated his intention to impose an additional tariff of 10%, effective February 1, on eight EU countries that oppose his plan to acquire Greenland. The increase would rise to 25% by June if an agreement to purchase the autonomous territory of Denmark is not reached. The EU, led by Germany, has threatened to strike back with tariffs of approximately $100 billion on US imports, targeting agriculture and food products, consumer goods, and a wide range of manufactured goods. They are also in the process of freezing the trade deal struck in June of last year. Futures markets indicate a sharply lower open for the major US market indexes. How do we play this as investors?

I think we have to use last April as a playbook. Given that the EU is not backing down from these threats, it increases the likelihood that a compromise will be reached. There is minimal support in Congress for annexing Greenland, and the majority of the voting public in the US is against it as well, based on recent polling. Markets would likely cascade, as they did last April, in the event of further escalation. That might be all it takes for a de-escalation, as we saw last year. The focus should be on what is most likely to happen rather than what we think or want to happen. In my view, ultimately, markets dictate policy. That amount of damage done depends on how quickly policy responds. Last April, it took at 19% correction in the S&P 500 index. I doubt it will be anything close to that this time around.

There is also the issue of the legality of such tariffs, which is currently being determined by the Supreme Court. The Trump administration has indicated that it would use the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) of 1977 to impose these tariffs, as it has with the majority of the other tariffs in force. The rationale in this case is that annexing Greenland is a national emergency because of the risk that China or Russia might seize the territory. Treasury Secretary Bessent has stated that the “national emergency is to avoid a national emergency.” Then there are personal vendettas the President has identified he wants to settle with tariffs. There is no definitive reasoning which complicates the matter.

Regardless, I think it is highly probable that the Supreme Court rules against the use of the IEEPA to impose the tariffs currently in force, which would then limit the ability to use them under current circumstances. The decision could come as soon as this week, and Polymarket odds indicate a 70% probability that the Supreme Court will rule against their use under this law. Other measures may be sought to impose tariffs, but such a ruling would clearly have a measurable impact and likely force the administration down other avenues of dispute resolution.

My bottom line is that this latest development is unlikely to cause anything other than a temporary pullback in markets, which I would view as another healthy pause to consolidate gains and refresh the uptrend. This is largely because of how strong the economic and market fundamentals are today.

The rate of economic growth is expected to be north of 5% in the fourth quarter, and despite a nearly 2% contribution coming from the improvement in the trade deficit we still have consumer spending increasing at 3% rate. Additionally, we have robust fiscal stimulus in effect, as households are scheduled to receive $100-$150 billion in additional tax refunds from last year, due to retroactive cuts from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) that were not reflected in withholdings. Then there is an increase in capital spending likely to occur due to the restoration of 100% bonus depreciation for qualified business equipment. These are very strong tailwinds to economic growth.

The latest inflation reading for December showed the core rate falling to a three-year low of 2.6%. This paves the way for more rate cuts from the Federal Reserve, either sooner or later, depending on the influence the new Fed Chair has in May.

Meanwhile, corporate profits are expected to post double-digit gains in 2026. Most importantly, the small-cap universe is leading the charge in terms of profit growth, which is a strong endorsement for continued domestic economic growth. According to data aggregator FactSet, earnings are expected to grow 15% for the S&P 500 this year. Better yet, the S&P 400 mid-cap index is expected to realize growth of 17%, while the S&P 600 small-cap index is forecast to produce 19% growth. The Russell 2000 small-cap index, which includes companies with no profits, is expected to realize an increase in earnings of more than 60%. The accuracy of these numbers is not as important as the rates of change. That rate of change is what drives stock price performance, and it should be the overarching theme for 2026.

Stock prices track closely with earnings growth over time, and the strength in the growth rate that we are seeing as we move from larger to smaller tells us that it is broadening dramatically. This explains the improvement in market breadth that led the Russell 2000 index to outperform the S&P 500 for 11 days in a row through the end of last week. It is easy to allow our opinions about geopolitical developments to dictate our outlooks for whether markets will outperform or underperform, and few are as controversial as the one we are dealing with now, but markets are unbiased and fundamentally based discounting mechanisms of future economic activity, and as a market strategist, I must be too. In a worst case scenario, I see the S&P 500 pulling back 5% before the uptrend resumes.

data.title: Wolters Kluwer: A High-Quality Company Discounted By AI
data.description:
Wolters Kluwer (OTC:WOLTF) is a Dutch software and professional services company. Wolters Kluwer has an excellent track record of growth, profitability, and return on capital. Due to the fears about disruption by artificial intelligence, the shares have declined heavily. Despite the fears and weaker U.S. dollar, Wolters Kluwer has been posting strong revenue and profit growth, backed by an excellent track record, dividends, and buybacks.

Company Overview

Wolters Kluwer posts an annual revenue of approximately €6 billion, of which 84% is recurring. The company has five diverse divisions serving health, tax, accounting, risk & compliance, and legal professionals. Corporate Performance & ESG is the newest division formed in March 2023 by combining four existing software businesses under the divisional banner. The company has 21,700 employees.

The company offers expert information, software, and services. For example, one-fifth of its revenue comes from recurring software sales. Geographically, 64% of its sales came from North America, 28% from Europe, and 8% from Asia Pacific and the rest of the world. Wolters Kluwer typically acquires 2-5 businesses each year. Due to growth by acquisitions, it operates under many different brands.

In 2024, Wolters Kluwer enjoyed 6% organic growth and 11% adjusted EPS growth. In the first nine months of 2025, the company reported 7% organic growth, and adjusted operating profit increased 15% in constant currency. Wolters Kluwer reaffirmed the 2025 outlook, including margin expansion, flat free cash flow, and mid- to high-single-digit EPS growth.

Threat of AI

Investors are afraid that AI will eat into Wolters Kluwer’s customers and applications. The tech giants and AI-native companies are targeting the most lucrative industries, such as healthcare. OpenAI and Anthropic have recently launched their health products for medical applications. Microsoft’s Dragon Copilot is another potential threat. These three are solving other types of problems; generally speaking, their primary task, for now, is different from what Wolters Kluwer’s UpToDate does.

Wolters Kluwer’s medical product called UpToDate brings in 10% of the company’s revenue. It has 3 million users and 7600 expert contributors. To some extent, UpToDate includes proprietary content and is reviewed by professionals. This should create trust and additional value for the users. Since Wolters Kluwer’s customers and users are experts in their respective fields, one could argue that the integrity of the system and accuracy of the information play a crucial role. Its subscription services enjoy 90% renewal rates.

Naturally, Wolters Kluwer has developed and is developing its own AI-powered products and integrating AI functionalities into existing ones. In 2024 the company spent €660 million on product development. On the 8th of December, Wolters Kluwer organized an “Investor Teach-In” on artificial intelligence, demonstrating its experience and progress in AI. It has been working with AI for over a decade and, for example, launched UpToDate Generative Labs in October 2023.

In addition to product development, many of its recent acquisitions have been companies that are focused on AI applications. Most recently, Wolters Kluwer acquired Libra GmbH, which provides AI technology for legal professionals. In the beginning of 2025, the company made a large acquisition of Brightflag for €425 million in the same application area. Furthermore, in the past decade, Wolters Kluwer has transformed itself, which made the majority of its revenue from print materials into a digital world. AI is another potential disruptor, but Wolters Kluwer is actively managing it.

Excellent Track Record of Profitable Growth

The share price of Wolters Kluwer has halved since its peak one year ago. The last time the stock was trading at its current level was in mid-2022. Since then the company has grown its revenue from €5 billion to €6 billion and net income from €820 million to €1.1 billion. At the same time, margins have inched higher.

Wolters Kluwer is a highly profitable business. Its EBIT and FCF margins have both averaged 20% in the past decade. Revenue has grown at a 5% rate, and earnings per share have grown at a pace of 11%. Wolters Kluwer has a strong balance sheet. Its net debt to EBITDA ratio stands at 2.2x. The indebtedness is slightly higher than usual due to several acquisitions and buybacks. In 2024, the ratio stood at 1.6x.

Wolters Kluwer will face a change of leadership in 2026 as long-tenured CEO Nancy McKinstry retires. She will be replaced by the CEO of the Health division, Stacey Caywood. Her career at Wolters Kluwer started in 1992. The company’s future has been and will continue to be in experienced hands. Recently, a British quality-focused fund, Fundsmith, established a position in Wolters Kluwer. Although the fund has underperformed for many years, an endorsement by its famous fund manager, Terry Smith, is a promising sign.

Valuation is Attractive by Historically Conservative Assumptions

The share price decline has compressed Wolters Kluwer’s multiples significantly below their historical averages. Its forward P/E multiple stands at 16x while it has averaged 23x over the past decade. P/FCF-multiple is 14.2x, and the 10-year average is over 20x. Its financial performance, margins, and returns on capital are all at their highest levels in history. Markets have priced in something that has not yet materialized. Analysts still expect earnings to grow at an annual pace of 10% in the next couple of years.

Analysts are expecting earnings per share of €5.26 for 2025. If we assume that the threat of AI is overestimated and Wolters Kluwer will be able to grow earnings at an annual pace of 8%, two percentage points lower than historically, the stock appears to be trading below its fair value. An ROIC of 18% and the assumed earnings growth should justify a P/E multiple higher than 20x. A 10% discount rate and a dividend growing in line with earnings result in a fair value of €102. An 8% discount rate would lead to a fair value of €121.

There are several other companies and stocks that have experienced similar sell-offs as Wolters Kluwer. Gartner (IT) and RELX (RELX) are fairly good peers. A comparison gives a rather neutral picture of Wolters Kluwer’s valuation. Based on earnings, the stock appears attractively valued, taking into account the prevailing financial performance. That could indicate that the price decline has gone a bit too far.

Company

Wolters Kluwer

The Company is Returning Cash at an Accelerated Pace

Wolters Kluwer pays a semi-annual dividend. For the fiscal year 2024, the company paid a total dividend of €2.33 per share (€0.83+€1.5). For the fiscal year 2025, it is likely to pay the final dividend in May. The payout ratio stands comfortably below 50%. It has maintained or increased the dividend for 30 years, and it aims to pay a progressive dividend. The dividend increases have continued for nine consecutive years at an annual rate of 10%.

In November 2025, the company completed a €1 billion share repurchase program and has been authorized for an additional €200 million of buybacks. That represents 5.7% of the less than €21 billion market cap. The company has executed the program throughout 2025. Between 2020 and 2024, the company has bought back 35.6 million shares. In hindsight, it paid dearly for 6.4 million shares in 2024 as repurchases were made at an average price of €149. Now, the company is delightfully taking a stance behind the depressed share price with additional repurchases.

Conclusion

Wolters Kluwer is a high-quality company facing a new threat that is potentially overestimated by the market. The company has an excellent track record of profitable growth and successful transformations and is led by experienced leaders. In addition to rebound potential, the shares offer a decent dividend yield, and earnings are boosted by the buybacks. Although the threat of AI requires close monitoring, the valuation appears attractive.

The company will report its full year 2025 results on February 25th.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The eIPP Effect: Why Archer Aviation's Regulatory Path Now Looks Clearer Than Ever
data.description:
Thesis: A clearer regulatory framework

Archer Aviation (ACHR) has made a move to launch air taxi trials across quite a few U.S. cities under the White House’s newly established eVTOL Integration Pilot Program (eIPP). It's a pretty big inflexion point for both the company and the broader advanced air mobility space in general. Now, the first thing this does for Archer is that it aligns their initial commercial plans with a federal framework. It’s actually set to be the first of its kind at scale. A coordinated, federally sanctioned pathway that can move eVTOL aircraft from testing/certification into a real-world environment like city-level operations. And the best thing is that it comes with a much clearer regulatory framework for Archer to follow, albeit not fully finalised.

You see, the program is established by executive order and led by the U.S. Department of Transportation in coordination with the FAA. So it’s designed to accelerate these early air taxi deployments by bringing together federal regulators with the state and local governments to see what they really want from U.S. eVTOL manufacturers before launching in public/private partnerships. Prior to having the eIPP, there was a lot of regulatory uncertainty for Archer, centred around how eVTOLs would actually operate once certified. They had multiple questions around airspace access and how they would interact with their existing air traffic control systems. And that's because eVTOL integration is still at a really early stage and a whole new technology for many of these cities' local authorities. I think they want the Midnight to come to their cities and see the appeal/upside in what Archer can bring; they don’t want to be left with room for error. There are vertiport infrastructure standards, emergency response coordination, even noise rules, and local government approvals that remain pretty fragmented and largely untested at scale. So companies like Archer, which can design and certify aircraft, now have a unified mechanism to validate their models in live urban environments under this program.

The eIPP essentially addresses the gap by allowing a few selected companies, like Archer, to conduct some controlled, early operations with explicit federal support. So this should give the regulators an opportunity to observe how the Midnight can operate in real-world performance. I think once they see this, a lot of questions can be answered, and we will see these cities prepare their infrastructure and policies in parallel with what players like Archer are doing. And keep in mind that this effect should become amplified since it is being combined with the DOT’s newly released National AAM Strategy. They, on the other hand, want to synchronise the whole certification process, from building operations to infrastructure development, and eventually early deployment, so it brings it all into a single national roadmap rather than a series of disconnected approvals, which we used to see Archer hop from. So now, I see Archer having a bit less execution risk since they now have clarity on the ‘last mile’ between aircraft certification and commercial service. The timing is also critical since Archer are nearing some pretty key certification milestones and planning for operations ahead of the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics. I see it as the eIPP and National AAM Strategy effectively converting years of policy ambiguity into an actionable launch framework for the company.

Archer is also participating here through multiple public/private partnerships, which include operations in California, Texas, Florida, Georgia, and New York. So they’re clearly going with a deliberate, city-by-city approach to market entry that prioritises local infrastructure readiness. Their goal here is to gain operational credibility first on these stages and improve their public safety image. For example, if we take the exclusive application with Huntington Beach, it goes to show how strong Archer’s position in the Los Angeles region is and also directly supports its role as the Official Air Taxi Provider for the 2028 Olympic Games. So there's a clear path from here, from pilot programs to globally visible commercial operations. Strategically speaking, we want the initiative to help set up Archer not just as an aircraft manufacturer, but as an operator tied to federal, state, and municipal planning. I'm expecting this to give them a first-mover advantage whilst still giving the image to investors and regulators that the industry has moved decisively from just experimental testing into execution. The main goal being show us there are early revenue-generating flight operations taking place.

The role of Nvidia IGX Thor

As you know, Archer has just announced at CES 2026 that it’s set to build the next gen of aviation-focused artificial intelligence by using Nvidia’s IGX Thor platform. It's a pretty big step in the right direction in the maturation of eVTOL. I also expect the platform to significantly advance its air mobility tech, as I'll explain. For those that don’t know, the IGX Thor platform is a pretty high-performance, safety-certified edge AI computing system. It's specifically designed for real-time, mission-critical environments, which means it can deliver massive amounts of AI processing power directly onboard vehicles/machines. So this should enable things like advanced perception, sensor fusion, decision-making, and predictive analytics with extremely low latency. It’s also built to meet safety and determinism requirements, which makes it ideal for domains such as aviation, robotics, and autonomous systems, where Archer just can’t afford failures since AI has to operate continuously under strict operational constraints.

Now, in using this edge AI, Archer is hoping to get a core, safety-critical system underpinning pilot awareness, not just a peripheral enhancement. What I mean by this is that Archer is now essentially designing their aircraft so that AI is embedded at the heart of how the actual aircraft operates, rather than just some add-on used only for limited features or post-flight analysis. Now, these AI systems are tipped to continuously process data from sensors to give navigation systems and airspace inputs in real time during flights. For example, the basic things would, of course, be detecting potential conflicts, such as nearby aircraft, unexpected obstacles, or updates on weather conditions. They also come in the form of clear, prioritised alerts before a human might notice them. Now, in terms of airspace integration, AI can also dynamically adjust an eVTOL’s routing and timing to operate far more safely within a crowded urban airspace. This aspect should help Archer’s aircraft coexist with helicopters/commercial jets whilst also complying with air traffic control requirements. The planned deployment for the system is at Hawthorne Municipal Airport, which is now owned by Archer and tipped to be the operational heart of its Los Angeles air taxi network. So in my mind, this should create a rare, vertically integrated test environment where Archer can use it in real-world flight operations, manufacturing and pilot training.

The competition

Now, under the new regulatory framework created by eIPP and the DOT’s National AAM Strategy, I think we’re seeing Archer’s go-to-market approach starting to meaningfully diverge from, and, in some respects, de-risk itself relative to their competition, such as Joby Aviation (JOBY). Archer is pursuing here for a federally aligned, city-partnered rollout strategy. So they want to embed themselves directly into public-private partnerships that involve the likes of the DOT, FAA, and municipal governments from the outset. When you look at Joby’s more vertically integrated, operator-first model, it's somewhat of a contrast. Joby has historically emphasised internal control over aircraft development, operations, and services. And they often advance through bilateral FAA engagement rather than a coordinated, city-led federal framework. It's also no secret that Joby has made pretty strong progress on certification and operational readiness, but now with Archer’s eIPP participation, they also have a pathway for validation.

Most importantly, Archer’s strategy now aligns very closely with how regulators and cities want eVTOLs to be introduced. They need them coming in incrementally, transparently, and with shared responsibility for things like infrastructure, safety, and public acceptance. The alignment is even stronger when you consider that Archer is going to play the role of the Official Air Taxi Provider at the Olympic Games. I see Archer using that publicity as a globally visible anchor. Somewhat of a use case that no peer currently matches. I also expect them to use it as a fixed commercialisation deadline around which the regulators can coordinate.

Risk

One near-term I see for Archer is still around regulatory scrutiny. And that's because of how swift things can change from a rule point of view. Just recently, Congress made the move to tighten aviation safety rules because of a fatal military helicopter crash. Proposed changes to the National Defense Authorisation Act (NDAA) would remove exemptions that currently allow certain aircraft to operate without full ADS-B surveillance. So that would signal a broader shift toward much stricter and more uniform airspace safety requirements. Now, Archer’s eVTOL aircraft already incorporate ADS-B systems, as you know, but the concern for investors is that tougher rules now could indirectly raise certification and operational hurdles, particularly since we’re seeing the industry move toward increasingly autonomous flight capabilities.

Valuation

As you would expect, Archer’s current valuation reflects its status as a pre-revenue, high-growth aviation tech company rather than a traditional industrial firm. The stock is trading around $8.86, and they carry a market cap of roughly $6.5 billion with an enterprise value of about $4.9 billion. They’re also supported by a pretty strong balance sheet, which includes $1.64 billion in cash and only $88.6 million in total debt. Now, because the company is still loss-making, a lot of traditional ratios are not yet meaningful, making asset- and sales-based metrics much more relevant for Archer stock. On that basis, the company trades at about 2.0x forward price to sales and 2.6x forward price to book. I think these are quite reasonable levels relative to other early-stage aerospace and advanced mobility peers, especially given their long runway of cash and improving loss trajectory. Overall, the valuation would suggest investors are pricing in execution and certification progress over the next few years, which should serve as the biggest stock catalyst.

Just to swing back briefly to the competition, Joby's valuation, on the other hand, reflects a much more aggressive growth premium than Archer's. With the stock now trading around $15.43, Joby has a market cap of about $14.1 billion and an enterprise value of about $13.1 billion, despite them still being a pre-revenue company. The most relevant comparison here is sales/asset-based multiples, in which Joby appears very expensive. They trade at around 330x forward EV to sales and over 8.5x forward price-to-book. So this kind of premium tells me investors are pricing in Joby as the long-term category leader between the two, with faster certification, earlier autonomy, and probably more global scaling potential.

Looking ahead, next earnings

Next earnings for Archer are set to come in sometime around February 26th, and I think we should see the same trend typical for this pre-commercial eVTOL developer. Consensus estimates are showing a loss per share of about –$0.23 to –$0.24, which would be a slight improvement compared with the larger losses we’ve seen in prior years. It’s reflective of a bit of incremental progress toward product development and cost discipline, but still well below breakeven. Revenue expectations for the 4Q25 quarter are forecast to be roughly $1.4 million. I also expect many investors/analysts will likely be watching not just the headline numbers, but updates on progress toward certification of the Midnight.

data.title: AMD: Regret Comes Later (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
AMD (AMD) stock has been on a tear in the last 12 months, and while since my last analysis shares are relatively flat, they may indeed still go higher in price in the short term. But that is no reason to get involved right now as a value investor. Markets are overvalued. In the current macroeconomy, based on aggregate market valuation that takes into account 14 market-leading models, the historical statistical likelihood of a positive three-year (let alone five-year) S&P 500 (SP500) return is zero. I'll say that again: historically, since the 1950s, at this market valuation, there have been zero instances of a positive three-year annual return in the S&P 500. This also tracks most of the time when the models are independent and not aggregated. Ladies and gentlemen, that is playing in a game where the house always wins: arguably 100% of the time. The question is, are you the sucker at the table?

If you're asking yourself if this time is different, you're asking the wrong question. You need to be asking yourself, "is this a game I want to play?" In other words, to protect your future, you must know how to analyze correctly. Failure in financial markets to correctly assess situations more often than not leads to ruin; it is the same in life more broadly. And the regret comes later, not when everything feels euphoric, but when the good emotions fade, reality sets in, and we have to live with the often generational consequences of our present perspectives which we wrongly or rightly made decisions on.

When I look at AMD, I see a genuinely remarkable company and CEO. But I also see a crowd of market participants that have projected their value onto the stock to the detriment of the equity's intrinsic valuation being accurately depicted in public markets. This is a shame, but as we know, it is not uncommon.

Truthfully, we're in a capex cycle that is near-peak in terms of equity market sentiment, even if the real-capex peak may be two or three more years away. Given that markets price equities about 12 months prior to real economy activity, we are looking at about one to two years of stock market growth left in the AI supercycle's phase 1, which I call "scarcity overbuild."

What happens next looks like return on investment slippage, weaker utilization, pushouts, and even pricing pressure in cloud services and inference. The capex cycle's equity market downturn does not require a collapse in demand, it simply requires the market's risk-premium to adjust due to the reality of cash economics. When terminal return on invested capital projections reset, valuation multiples will compress. As a result, AMD's stock price will tank hard, and likely become undervalued, before the full utilization economics are proven.

Everything Flashes Green, Until It Doesn't

In every bubble throughout modern-era market history, it's been consensus bullishness right through to the point of collapse. If you want evidence of this, just look at AMD's own consensus Wall Street price targets during the 2021–2022 inflation bubble:

There are several fundamental elements that are likely to cause a value de-rating beyond pure valuation. You need to be monitoring capex leases as a share of operating cash flow, depreciation growth versus revenue, cost per compute function (inference vs. training), and power constraints. Each of which I have discussed in detail in prior analyses on my Seeking Alpha profile page.

Remember, everything flashes green in a bubble because consensus is driving the show. Everybody agrees that the opportunity is ongoing, and next year will bring greater rewards than last. But people who listen to consensus will always end up one way in the end: average. Instead, someone who is truly skilled and attuned to their innate abilities will recognize that the valuation is too high for a stock precisely because consensus is "unwaveringly bullish." If you cannot trust your own perceptions, you will be left following the guidance of others, who may not have your best interests at heart.

Could This Time Be Different?

There are so many issues with the argument that this time is different. We're not looking at an undervalued market (i.e. a market that has changed from being previously overvalued), and making a rational judgement that entry points are now opportune. We are, instead, looking at the same market with the same disastrous symptoms of impending loss and decline, and questioning if factors are materially different. There is a reason that history is the study of statesmen and financiers alike. As Churchill said, "Study history, study history. In history lies all the secrets of statecraft."

From my study of historical cyclical price action that envelopes intrinsic value, I believe the following outlook is most reasonable to assume right now. That is, over the next 9–12 months, AMD stock will test new highs. Following this "melt-up," as it becomes clearer that hyperscalers (who are the primary customers of AMD's data-center semiconductors) will be refining spending in the years ahead, AMD stock will begin to consolidate and then sell off.

I can't predict the exact timing of such a correction; no one can. I have made my best estimates. However, I am confident in the general trend we are likely to witness, which makes this a good time to begin trimming AMD & AI-semi positions, and then accumulate shares again at the next value opportunity once the trough of the correction looks settled.

In the following chart, the white line represents the real price action that I forecast, while the orange line represents real intrinsic value estimated by me.

Q4 Earnings Preview

In such an overvalued macro, no long-term value investor should care more about whether AMD beats Q4 estimates than whether forward demand confirms current certainty. Even if confirmation is provided, management won't be providing guidance at this juncture for two years out, which is when the phase-1 thesis begins to break.

At worst, we're likely looking at in-line results leading to consolidation in price and some nervousness in the broader markets. More likely, we're looking at a mild beat with a moderate raise aligning with prudent optical support. Either way, nothing massive is on the agenda that's public knowledge right now that would force a positive structural re-rate. And with sentiment being full, this is not a moment to invest, only to hold and trim accordingly in the near future.

AMD's Q4 guidance is 25% year-over-year revenue growth and 4% sequentially at the midpoint. Non-GAAP gross margin is forecast at 54.5%, and guidance excluded MI308 shipments to China.

Conclusion: Hold

Have you ever crashed and burned? If you're young, maybe not. If you're old and lucky, maybe not. But the ego always feels the safest, the most enjoyed, just before reality sets in. This is not a time in the markets for complacency. It's a time where you either know clearly what you are doing, or you don't.

The AI boom looks impenetrable. GDP in America is soaring beyond expectations, and capex looks "eternal." If you've any wisdom in you, you will recognize that nothing is eternal, but God.

A consensus rating of +23.5% upside in the next 12 months means nothing in the face of an aggregate market valuation that is more than +2 standard deviations above fair value. It is statistically unknown for this valuation matrix to deliver positive total returns over the next three to five years. Assess the current dynamics how you want, even own a small weight in AI shares, but if you aren't lowering beta right now and raising a cash position, all you are doing is driving with blind optimism down the road to the inevitable crash.

data.title: Hartford Balanced Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities advanced in the fourth quarter, registering a sizable return for 2025. Markets were bolstered by robust corporate earnings, resilient consumer spending, and solid economic growth, although anxiety about lofty valuations in the technology sector and concerns surrounding massive AI spending drove capital toward value/cyclical areas of the market. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) reduced interest rates by 50 basis points (bps)[1] during the quarter.

Global fixed-income markets also delivered positive total returns in the fourth quarter of 2025, as policy uncertainty, fiscal developments, and divergent central-bank actions shaped the investment landscape. The US began the quarter with its longest government shutdown on record, which delayed key economic data and forced the Fed to make policy decisions with limited visibility. The shutdown ended in mid-November, but data gaps persisted, adding to market uncertainty. Credit spreads[2] diverged across ratings and regions. In the US, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and high-yield credit spreads tightened, while investment-grade spreads widened. European spreads broadly narrowed. Both US investment-grade and high-yield posted positive total returns, outperforming European counterparts. Global aggregate and US high-yield indices contributed positively to returns, while government bond returns were more mixed.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Balanced Income Fund (I share) underperformed its benchmark.
  • Sector allocation was the main driver of relative underperformance, driven by our underweight to communication services and overweights to real estate and utilities, but was partially offset by our overweight to healthcare and underweight to consumer discretionary.
  • Security selection contributed to relative performance, driven by strong selection in healthcare, materials, and financials, and partially offset by selection in information technology.
  • An overweight to and security selection within high-yield credit and an overweight to emerging-markets debt contributed positively to relative returns over the period.
  • Security selection within emerging-markets debt had a slightly negative effect on relative performance.

Positioning & Outlook

  • Our universe remains rich with new opportunities. We continue to focus on finding high quality businesses with strong balance sheets and sustainable dividends. We’re spending a lot of time on our downside stress-test scenarios and are confident in the sustainability of dividends and long-term value of holdings in the portfolio.
  • Within equities, utilities, healthcare, and real estate represented our largest sector overweights relative to the benchmark at quarter-end. We were most underweight to communication services, information technology, and financials.
  • Within the fixed-income portion of the Fund, we maintain our underweight positioning in investment-grade credit as valuations remain relatively rich compared to history. We maintain a modest overweight to emerging markets because we find attractive relative value in this segment of the market.

[1] Basis point is a unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1% and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security

[2] Spreads are the difference in yields between two fixed income securities with the same maturity but originating from different investment sectors.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Share Class Inception: A, Y - 7/31/06; F - 2/28/17; I - 2/26/10; R3, R4, R5 - 5/28/10; R6 - 11/7/14. Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 7/31/06. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Benchmark: The Blended Index consists of 45% Russell 1000 Value Index, 44% Bloomberg US Corporate Index, 5.5% JP Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index Plus, and 5.5% Bloomberg US High Yield 2% Issuer Cap Index. Indices are unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

BLOOMBERG® and any Bloomberg Index are service marks of Bloomberg Finance L.P. and its affiliates, including Bloomberg Index Services Limited ("BISL"), the administrator of the indices (collectively, "Bloomberg") and have been licensed for use for certain purposes by Hartford Funds. Bloomberg is not affiliated with Hartford Funds, and Bloomberg does not approve, endorse, review, or recommend any Hartford Funds product. Bloomberg does not guarantee the timeliness, accurateness, or completeness of any data or information relating to Hartford Funds products.

[1] Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • The portfolio managers may allocate a portion of the Fund's assets to specialist portfolio managers, and among different asset classes, each of which may not work as intended. • Fixed income security risks include credit, liquidity, call, duration, event and interest rate risk. As interest rates rise, bond prices generally fall. • For dividend-paying stocks, dividends are not guaranteed and may decrease without notice. • Value investing style may go in and out of favor, which may cause the Fund to underperform other funds that use different investing styles. • Foreign investments may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments. • Restricted securities may be more difficult to sell and price than other securities. • Derivatives are generally more volatile and sensitive to changes in market or economic conditions than other securities; their risks include currency, leverage, liquidity, index, pricing, valuation, and counterparty risk.

Investors should carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in a fund's full prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by visiting hartfordfunds.com. Please read it carefully before investing.

Mutual funds are distributed by Hartford Funds Distributors, LLC (HFD), Member FINRA. Advisory services are provided by Hartford Funds Management Company, LLC (HFMC). Certain funds are sub-advised by Wellington Management Company LLP. HFMC and Wellington Management are SEC registered investment advisers. HFD and HFMC are not affiliated with any sub-adviser.
